title: Sudden Hind Leg Paralysis in Cats: Emergency Guide and Triage
meta_desc: If your cat is suddenly dragging its back legs, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about causes like FATE and why you must see a vet immediately.
content_html:
What is Hind Leg Paralysis?

When a cat suddenly loses the ability to use its back legs, it is often due to a condition called Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (FATE), or a 'saddle thrombus.' This occurs when a blood clot, usually originating from the heart, travels through the aorta and lodges where the arteries branch off to the hind limbs. It can also be caused by sudden spinal cord trauma or severe disc herniation. The affected legs may feel cold to the touch and the paw pads may appear blue or pale.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. This is a high-priority medical emergency. Sudden hind leg weakness or dragging is extremely painful and life-threatening. You should not wait to see if the condition improves or wait for a standard appointment. Take your cat to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately, regardless of the time of day.

How a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a quick video of how your cat is moving—or a photo of their resting posture—can provide the veterinary triage team with vital information before you even arrive. Visual evidence allows the staff to quickly differentiate between neurological issues and vascular blockages, ensuring your cat receives the correct life-saving interventions the moment you walk through the door.

faqs.question: Is my cat in pain if they aren't crying?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even if a cat is not vocalizing, sudden paralysis is almost always associated with extreme pain and distress. Cats are masters at hiding pain as a survival instinct.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat aspirin for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human pain medications to cats. Many common over-the-counter drugs, such as Tylenol or Aspirin, are highly toxic to cats and can be fatal.

faqs.question: What are the survival rates for saddle thrombus?
faqs.answer: The prognosis varies based on the severity of the clot and the speed of treatment. Immediate aggressive veterinary care is the only way to improve the chances of recovery.
